_instructions: Append these entries to the 'entries' array in library.json. Also update '_meta.last_updated' to '2026-04-21'.

new_entries.title: Thornton Police Looking Into Complaint That Officer Ran Nearly 20,000 Flock Searches
new_entries.description: A community watchdog group, Thornton For All, filed an ethics complaint after discovering that a single Flock account identified as 'D. McT' had conducted 19,194 searches between October 2023 and December 2025, with only 29 tied to case numbers (fewer than 1%). The account was identified not through internal oversight but through public audit data on HaveIBeenFlocked.com — data released by other agencies in Flock's national network that did not redact short-name identifiers. The complaint also alleged searches outside normal working hours and repeated queries of the same plates. Additional concerns centered on a potential conflict of interest: the officer's spouse serves as the department's public information officer. Thornton PD acknowledged the complaint and announced new weekly auditing protocols with five trained employees; it previously relied on a single monthly audit by one administrator.

new_entries.title: ALPRs and Minneapolis' Growing Discriminatory Surveillance Dragnet
new_entries.description: Investigative report documenting MPD's deployment of 30 fixed Flock ALPR cameras across Minneapolis intersections, supplemented by approximately 70 additional privately-owned ALPRs at retail locations — nearly 100 cameras total surveilling city streets. In a two-month window (January–March 2026), these cameras logged data on more than 31 million vehicle passes, averaging over one million per intersection. Of those, only 12,446 generated a hot-list match. The piece examines the discriminatory geography of the installations and the gap between stated crime-fighting rationale and the mass collection of innocent motorist data, drawing on DeFlock.org mapping data.

new_entries.title: Former Orange County Officer Pleaded Guilty to Using Police Database to Track Mistress, Harassing Her with Thousands of Texts and Phone Calls
new_entries.description: Former Costa Mesa officer Robert Jay Josett, 35, pleaded guilty on April 14, 2026 to unauthorized computer access, harassment by repeated contact, and contempt of court for violating a restraining order. Between June and December 2023, while employed, Josett used the CLETS database to query 13 records on his mistress, her romantic interests, and his wife — none connected to his duties. He also accessed the department's Flock ALPR system to locate his mistress's vehicle and the vehicles of her partners. After being placed on leave in December 2023, he continued illegally accessing the Flock system to track her new boyfriend in Torrance. Over nine months he contacted her more than 100 times per day across calls, texts, and social media. He was sentenced to three years of informal probation and a 52-week domestic violence program.

new_entries.publication: WHRO / Virginia Center for Investigative Journalism
new_entries.title: Virginia Police Search Vehicle Surveillance Data 24/7. 'Why?' Isn't Always Clear.
new_entries.description: VCIJ at WHRO analyzed 200,000 Flock search logs from nearly 150 Virginia law enforcement agencies covering July–October 2025 — the first four months after Virginia's new ALPR law took effect. Despite the law requiring specific stated purposes for every search, officers routinely used abbreviations ('CJ,' 'inv,' 'T'), single-word reasons ('shooting,' 'investigation'), and descriptors like 'suspicious' or 'sus' with no further detail. More than 1,800 searches used vague 'suspicious' variants. The analysis also confirmed a January state report: Virginia agencies continued sharing data with 800 out-of-state agencies in apparent violation of the new law, with departments uncertain whether Flock had automatically disabled the sharing feature. Rights advocates argue the absence of real enforcement mechanisms renders the law structurally ineffective.

new_entries.title: No Permit, No Problem: Riverside County's 500+ Unauthorized Surveillance Cameras
new_entries.agency: Riverside County Sheriff's Department / Flock Safety
new_entries.description: A CPRA records request revealed that Riverside County issued three encroachment permits for 500+ Flock cameras, all to the wrong permittee: the Sheriff's Department received permits that Flock had applied for in its own name, improperly triggering a public-agency fee exemption that a private company should not have qualified for. The permits also lacked required traffic control plans for the majority of installations. Between the first and second permits, cameras operated for over 13 months with no valid permit at all. The county's records custodian certified under oath that no other authorizations exist. The investigation positions the Riverside case as representative of a nationwide pattern: Flock cameras routinely go up on public roads under permits that are unreviewed, improperly issued, or absent — with no agency taking responsibility for verification.
